AATBC.simple: AATBC is a long non-coding RNA, a type of RNA molecule that is not used to build a protein but instead may help regulate other genes. Its name reflects that it was first noticed in connection with bladder cancer, where it appears to be more active. Exactly how it works is still being studied, so its precise role should be considered uncertain.
AATBC.technical: AATBC (apoptosis associated transcript in bladder cancer) is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) originally identified as differentially expressed in bladder cancer. lncRNAs of this kind can act as regulators of gene expression, and AATBC has been reported in the literature in the context of tumor cell proliferation and apoptosis, but a single well-established molecular mechanism is not firmly defined. Its specific functional pathway and validated targets remain incompletely characterized and should be treated as not yet established.

ABCC13.simple: ABCC13 is a pseudogene, meaning it is a relic of a gene from the ABC transporter family that has lost the ability to make a working protein. In other animals related genes encode proteins that pump molecules across cell membranes, including some linked to multidrug resistance, but the human version is now thought to be non-functional. It is therefore not expected to produce a useful protein product.
ABCC13.technical: ABCC13 is annotated as a pseudogene within the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter superfamily, specifically related to the MRP/ABCC subfamily associated with multidrug resistance. Although the locus produces alternatively spliced transcripts, the human gene is considered incapable of encoding a functional ABC transporter protein. As a pseudogene it is not expected to yield a functional product, though pseudogene-derived transcripts can in principle have regulatory effects; no such role is well established here.

ADARB1.simple: ADARB1 makes an enzyme (also known as ADAR2) that edits RNA molecules by chemically changing certain letters of the genetic code after the RNA is made. One important job is editing the RNA for a brain glutamate receptor, which fine-tunes how nerve cells respond to signals. This kind of RNA editing is essential for normal nervous system function.
ADARB1.technical: ADARB1 encodes ADAR2, an adenosine deaminase acting on RNA that catalyzes site-specific adenosine-to-inosine (A-to-I) editing of double-stranded RNA. A well-established substrate is the pre-mRNA of the glutamate receptor subunit GRIA2 (GluR-B), where editing at the Q/R site alters receptor calcium permeability; the enzyme also autoedits its own pre-mRNA to create a new splice site. Alternative splicing yields multiple transcript variants differing in an Alu cassette insert and C-terminal length. A-to-I editing by ADAR2 is critical for normal neuronal function, and dysregulation has been implicated in neurological disease.

ANKRD20A11P.simple: ANKRD20A11P is a pseudogene, a leftover copy of a gene in the ankyrin repeat domain family that is not expected to make a working protein. Pseudogenes resemble real genes but have typically lost their original function. Its role, if any, is not well characterized.
ANKRD20A11P.technical: ANKRD20A11P is a pseudogene of the ANKRD20A (ankyrin repeat domain 20 family member A) gene family. As a pseudogene it is generally not expected to encode a functional protein product. Any regulatory function for its transcript has not been established and should be regarded as uncharacterized.

ANKRD30BP2.simple: ANKRD30BP2 is a pseudogene related to the ANKRD30B gene, meaning it is a non-functional copy that is not expected to make a working protein. Pseudogenes look like genes but have usually lost their activity. Its biological role, if it has one, is not well understood.
ANKRD30BP2.technical: ANKRD30BP2 is a pseudogene (ANKRD30B pseudogene 2) derived from the ANKRD30B locus. Being a pseudogene, it is not expected to produce a functional protein. No well-established regulatory or biological function has been defined for this locus, so its role should be considered uncharacterized.

ASMER1.simple: ASMER1 is a long non-coding RNA, a type of RNA that does not code for a protein but may help regulate gene activity. Its name links it to fat (adipocyte) cells and metabolism. The specific details of what it does are not firmly established.
ASMER1.technical: ASMER1 (adipocyte associated metabolic related lncRNA 1) is a long non-coding RNA implicated by its annotation in adipocyte biology and metabolic regulation. lncRNAs in this category may modulate gene expression in adipose tissue, but a definitive, validated molecular mechanism for ASMER1 is not well established. Its specific targets and functional pathways remain to be characterized.

B3GALT5-AS1.simple: B3GALT5-AS1 is an antisense RNA, meaning it is transcribed from the opposite strand of DNA to the neighboring gene B3GALT5. Such RNAs can sometimes influence how their partner gene is turned on or off, but they do not make proteins themselves. Its specific role has not been well characterized.
B3GALT5-AS1.technical: B3GALT5-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the protein-coding gene B3GALT5. Antisense transcripts can regulate expression of their sense-strand partner through transcriptional interference, chromatin modification, or RNA-RNA interactions, but no specific mechanism has been firmly established for this locus. Its functional role relative to B3GALT5 remains uncharacterized.

BACH1.simple: BACH1 (in this annotation, the BRCA1-interacting helicase, also called BRIP1/FANCJ) makes a protein that helps repair damaged DNA. It works together with the breast cancer protein BRCA1 to fix breaks in both strands of DNA. Inherited changes in this gene can increase the risk of certain cancers.
BACH1.technical: Note: this entry's description corresponds to a protein-coding gene rather than a non-coding RNA. BACH1 (BRCA1 interacting DNA helicase 1; also known as BRIP1/FANCJ) encodes a RecQ-family DEAH-box helicase that binds the BRCT repeats of BRCA1. The BRCA1-BACH1 complex contributes to BRCA1-dependent double-strand break repair, and germline mutations in this gene are associated with cancer predisposition and Fanconi anemia complementation group J.

BACH1-IT2.simple: BACH1-IT2 is an intronic transcript, an RNA produced from within the introns (the non-coding internal segments) of the BACH1 gene. RNAs like this often do not make proteins, and many have no clearly defined job. Its specific function has not been well characterized.
BACH1-IT2.technical: BACH1-IT2 is an intronic transcript (intronic transcript 2) arising from within the BACH1 gene locus. Such intronic non-coding transcripts frequently lack a defined function, and no well-established molecular role has been demonstrated for this transcript. Its biological significance should be regarded as uncharacterized.

BACH1-IT3.simple: BACH1-IT3 is an intronic transcript, an RNA made from within the introns of the BACH1 gene. Transcripts of this kind generally do not encode proteins and frequently have no established role. Its specific function is not well characterized.
BACH1-IT3.technical: BACH1-IT3 is an intronic transcript (intronic transcript 3) originating from within the BACH1 locus. Intronic non-coding RNAs of this type often have no demonstrated function, and no well-established mechanism or role has been defined for this transcript. Its biological significance remains uncharacterized.

BAGE2.simple: BAGE2 is a pseudogene belonging to the BAGE gene family, meaning it is a copy that is not expected to produce a working protein. Available annotation only predicts that any product would be found in the space outside cells. Its actual biological role is not well characterized.
BAGE2.technical: BAGE2 is annotated as a pseudogene within the BAGE (B melanoma antigen) family. Computational annotation predicts a possible extracellular localization, but as a pseudogene it is not expected to encode a functional protein. No well-established function has been demonstrated, so its biological role should be considered uncharacterized.

BNAT1.simple: BNAT1 is a natural antisense transcript, an RNA copied from the opposite DNA strand near another gene. Its name links it to breast cancer and to regulation of ESR1, the estrogen receptor gene. The exact way it acts is not firmly established and should be treated with caution.
BNAT1.technical: BNAT1 (breast cancer associated ESR1 regulating natural antisense transcript 1) is a long non-coding natural antisense transcript annotated in the context of breast cancer and ESR1 (estrogen receptor 1) regulation. Natural antisense transcripts can influence expression of nearby loci, and BNAT1 is named for a proposed link to ESR1 regulation, but a definitive validated mechanism is not firmly established. Its specific functional details remain to be fully characterized.

BRWD1-AS1.simple: BRWD1-AS1 is an antisense RNA, transcribed from the DNA strand opposite the BRWD1 gene. RNAs of this type do not make proteins and may or may not influence their neighboring gene. Its specific role has not been well characterized.
BRWD1-AS1.technical: BRWD1-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the BRWD1 locus. While antisense transcripts can modulate sense-gene expression, no specific mechanism has been firmly established for BRWD1-AS1. Its functional role remains uncharacterized.

BRWD1-AS2.simple: BRWD1-AS2 is a second antisense RNA produced from the strand opposite the BRWD1 gene. It does not encode a protein, and like many antisense RNAs its function is uncertain. Its specific role has not been well characterized.
BRWD1-AS2.technical: BRWD1-AS2 is a long non-coding antisense RNA (antisense RNA 2) transcribed opposite the BRWD1 locus. No specific regulatory mechanism for this transcript has been established. Its functional relationship to BRWD1 remains uncharacterized.

BTG3-AS1.simple: BTG3-AS1 is an antisense RNA, copied from the DNA strand opposite the BTG3 gene. Such RNAs do not produce proteins and sometimes help regulate their neighboring gene, though this is not always the case. Its specific function has not been well characterized.
BTG3-AS1.technical: BTG3-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the BTG3 gene. Antisense lncRNAs can regulate their sense-strand partners, but no specific, validated mechanism has been established for BTG3-AS1. Its functional role should be regarded as uncharacterized.

C21ORF62-AS1.simple: C21orf62-AS1 is an antisense RNA produced from the strand opposite a chromosome 21 gene (C21orf62, also called EPCIP). It does not code for a protein. Its biological role is not well characterized.
C21ORF62-AS1.technical: C21orf62-AS1 (EPCIP antisense RNA 1) is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the C21orf62/EPCIP locus on chromosome 21. No specific molecular mechanism or function has been established for this antisense transcript. Its role relative to its sense-strand partner remains uncharacterized.

C21ORF91-OT1.simple: C21orf91-OT1 is an overlapping transcript, an RNA whose sequence overlaps the chromosome 21 gene C21orf91. RNAs like this typically do not make proteins and often have no clearly defined function. Its specific role is not well characterized.
C21ORF91-OT1.technical: C21orf91-OT1 is a non-coding overlapping transcript (overlapping transcript 1) associated with the C21orf91 locus on chromosome 21. Overlapping non-coding transcripts of this type frequently lack a demonstrated function, and no well-established mechanism has been defined for this transcript. Its biological significance remains uncharacterized.

CBR1-AS1.simple: CBR1-AS1 is an antisense RNA, transcribed from the DNA strand opposite the CBR1 gene. It does not encode a protein, and although antisense RNAs can sometimes affect their neighboring gene, its specific role is uncertain. Its function has not been well characterized.
CBR1-AS1.technical: CBR1-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the CBR1 (carbonyl reductase 1) gene. No specific regulatory mechanism for this transcript has been firmly established. Its functional relationship to CBR1 remains uncharacterized.

CBR3-AS1.simple: CBR3-AS1 is an antisense RNA copied from the strand opposite the CBR3 gene. It does not make a protein, and like many antisense RNAs its precise job is uncertain. Its specific role has not been well characterized.
CBR3-AS1.technical: CBR3-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the CBR3 (carbonyl reductase 3) gene. Although antisense transcripts can influence sense-gene expression, no specific validated mechanism has been established for CBR3-AS1. Its functional role should be regarded as uncharacterized.

CBS.simple: CBS carries the instructions for an enzyme that helps the body process homocysteine, a building-block-related molecule, and convert it into other useful compounds. When this enzyme does not work properly, homocysteine builds up and causes a condition called homocystinuria, which can affect the eyes, bones, blood vessels, and brain. The same enzyme also helps produce hydrogen sulfide, a small signaling molecule inside cells.
CBS.technical: CBS is a protein-coding gene encoding cystathionine beta-synthase, which functions as a homotetramer to catalyze the conversion of homocysteine to cystathionine, the committed first step of the transsulfuration pathway. The enzyme uses pyridoxal 5'-phosphate as a cofactor and is allosterically activated by S-adenosyl-methionine, and it is a major contributor to cellular hydrogen sulfide production. Loss-of-function defects cause cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency (CBSD), the most common cause of classical homocystinuria. Multiple alternatively spliced transcript variants have been described.

CBSL.simple: CBSL is a gene closely related to CBS, the gene for the homocysteine-processing enzyme, and is found in a duplicated region of chromosome 21. Its name ('CBS like') reflects this similarity, but its own specific function has not been well characterized. It may encode a protein resembling the CBS enzyme, though this is not firmly established.
CBSL.technical: CBSL ('cystathionine beta-synthase like') is a CBS paralog located within a segmental duplication on chromosome 21 and is highly similar in sequence to CBS. No curated functional summary is available, and its specific biological role and the activity of any encoded product are not well characterized; any cystathionine beta-synthase-like enzymatic function should be regarded as inferred from homology rather than experimentally established. Because of its near-identity to CBS, it is a frequent source of read-mapping and annotation ambiguity in this duplicated region.

CFAP298-TCP10L.simple: This entry is not a normal single gene but a 'readthrough' transcript, produced when the cell's machinery transcribes through two neighboring genes (CFAP298 and TCP10L) without stopping in between. The resulting RNA combines parts of both genes and may encode a fusion protein sharing sequence with each. Such readthrough products are naturally occurring, and their independent biological importance is generally uncertain.
CFAP298-TCP10L.technical: CFAP298-TCP10L is a naturally occurring readthrough transcription locus spanning the neighboring CFAP298 (formerly C21orf59) and TCP10L genes on chromosome 21. The readthrough transcript may encode a fusion protein sharing sequence identity with each individual gene product. As with many conjoined/readthrough loci, the functional significance of the chimeric transcript distinct from the parent genes is not well established.

CH507-9B2.8.simple: This is an uncharacterized locus on chromosome 21, named after the genomic clone in which it was identified rather than for any known function. There is no curated information describing what, if anything, it does. Its biological role is unknown.
CH507-9B2.8.technical: CH507-9B2.8 is an uncharacterized clone-based locus on chromosome 21 with no curated functional annotation. Its RNA class, coding potential, and biological function are not established. It lies within a chromosome 21 region rich in segmental duplications, where annotation of such loci remains provisional.

CHODL-AS1.simple: CHODL-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA, a type of RNA that is not used to make a protein but may instead help regulate other genes. It is named for its position near the CHODL gene. Its specific function is not well characterized.
CHODL-AS1.technical: CHODL-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) gene; the description (an 'independently transcribed' lncRNA, NCBI LINC-style annotation) and its proximity to the CHODL locus suggest a possible regulatory relationship, but no curated functional summary is available. Its molecular mechanism, targets, and biological role are not well characterized, and any cis-regulatory effect on CHODL should be regarded as a hypothesis rather than an established function.

CLDN14-AS1.simple: CLDN14-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA, an RNA that does not code for a protein and that, in this case, is described in connection with a cell-signaling pathway (TGF-beta/SMAD3). Such RNAs can sometimes help fine-tune gene activity. Its precise function, however, is not firmly established.
CLDN14-AS1.technical: CLDN14-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) gene situated near CLDN14; its description references the TGF-beta/SMAD3 signaling pathway, implying a possible regulatory association. No curated mechanistic summary is available, so the specific molecular mechanism, direct interactors, and biological role remain not well characterized, and the suggested TGF-beta/SMAD3 link should be treated as provisional rather than mechanistically validated.

COL18A1-AS1.simple: COL18A1-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA, meaning it is transcribed from the opposite DNA strand of the COL18A1 collagen gene. Antisense RNAs can sometimes influence the activity of their neighboring gene, but this is not always the case. Its specific function has not been well characterized.
COL18A1-AS1.technical: COL18A1-AS1 is an antisense lncRNA transcribed from the strand opposite the COL18A1 (collagen XVIII) gene. No curated functional summary is available; its specific molecular mechanism and biological role are not well characterized. Any cis-acting regulation of COL18A1 expression is plausible given the antisense configuration but remains unestablished.

COL18A1-AS2.simple: COL18A1-AS2 is a second antisense long non-coding RNA associated with the COL18A1 collagen gene, transcribed from the opposite DNA strand. Like other antisense RNAs, it could potentially affect the neighboring gene, but its actual role is unknown. Its specific function has not been well characterized.
COL18A1-AS2.technical: COL18A1-AS2 is a second antisense lncRNA overlapping/adjacent to the COL18A1 locus on the opposite strand. No curated functional summary exists; its molecular mechanism, RNA-level interactions, and biological role are not well characterized. A regulatory relationship with COL18A1 is conceivable but not established.

CXADR.simple: CXADR makes a protein that sits on the surface of cells and acts as a docking site (receptor) that certain viruses, including coxsackieviruses and some adenoviruses, use to enter cells. It normally functions as a cell adhesion molecule, helping cells stick together. The gene also has several non-functional copies (pseudogenes) elsewhere in the genome.
CXADR.technical: CXADR is a protein-coding gene encoding the coxsackievirus and adenovirus receptor, a type I transmembrane cell-adhesion molecule of the immunoglobulin superfamily that serves as the entry receptor for group B coxsackieviruses and subgroup C adenoviruses. Several transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been described. Processed pseudogenes derived from this gene are present on chromosomes 15, 18, and 21.

CYP4F29P.simple: CYP4F29P is a pseudogene, a DNA sequence that resembles a working cytochrome P450 gene but has lost the ability to produce a functional enzyme. Because it is a pseudogene, it is not expected to make a normal protein product. Some pseudogenes can still be transcribed, but no regulatory role is established here.
CYP4F29P.technical: CYP4F29P is a pseudogene of the cytochrome P450 family 4, subfamily F. As a pseudogene it is not expected to produce a functional CYP4F enzyme, and no well-established regulatory function has been ascribed to it. Whether it is transcribed and any potential noncoding role are not established.

CYYR1.simple: CYYR1 encodes a small protein, rich in the amino acids cysteine and tyrosine, that is predicted to be located in cell membranes. Its precise biological function is not well understood. Available information is largely based on computational prediction rather than detailed experimental study.
CYYR1.technical: CYYR1 (cysteine and tyrosine rich 1) is a protein-coding gene whose product is predicted to localize to membranes. Its molecular function is not well characterized, and the available annotation is largely prediction-based rather than experimentally validated. It maps to chromosome 21 within the Down syndrome region, but no established disease mechanism is attributed to it.

CYYR1-AS1.simple: CYYR1-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA, transcribed from the opposite DNA strand of the CYYR1 gene. Antisense RNAs can sometimes regulate their neighboring gene, but this is not always so. Its specific function has not been well characterized.
CYYR1-AS1.technical: CYYR1-AS1 is an antisense lncRNA transcribed opposite the CYYR1 locus on chromosome 21. No curated functional summary is available; its molecular mechanism and biological role are not well characterized. Potential cis-regulation of CYYR1 is plausible from the antisense arrangement but not established.

D21S2088E.simple: D21S2088E is a genomic marker/locus designation on chromosome 21 (the 'D21S' prefix denotes a chromosome 21 DNA segment), not a well-characterized gene. There is no curated information describing a function for it. Its biological role, if any, is unknown.
D21S2088E.technical: D21S2088E is a chromosome 21 DNA segment locus identifier (D21S nomenclature, 'E' denoting an expressed sequence) rather than a functionally annotated gene. No curated functional summary is available, and its RNA class, coding potential, and biological role are not established. It is best regarded as a legacy/mapping-based locus designation.

DIP2A.simple: DIP2A makes a protein that is thought to play a role in guiding the growth and wiring of nerve fibers (axons) in the central nervous system. The gene is not produced at high levels in cells. Several slightly different versions of its protein can be made through alternative splicing.
DIP2A.technical: DIP2A (DIP2 acetate--CoA ligase A) is a protein-coding gene whose product is proposed to participate in axon patterning in the central nervous system. It is reported to be expressed at low levels, and several alternatively spliced transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been described. Its detailed molecular mechanism beyond these annotations is not extensively characterized.

DIP2A-IT1.simple: DIP2A-IT1 is an intronic transcript, a non-coding RNA produced from within an intron (a non-coding internal segment) of the DIP2A gene. Such transcripts often have no clearly defined function. Its specific role has not been well characterized.
DIP2A-IT1.technical: DIP2A-IT1 is an intronic transcript (a noncoding RNA arising from within an intron of the host gene DIP2A). No curated functional summary is available; its RNA-level mechanism and biological role are not well characterized. Intronic transcripts of this type frequently lack an established independent function.

DNMT3L-AS1.simple: DNMT3L-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA, transcribed from the opposite DNA strand of the DNMT3L gene. Antisense RNAs can sometimes influence their neighboring gene, but this is not guaranteed. Its specific function has not been well characterized.
DNMT3L-AS1.technical: DNMT3L-AS1 is an antisense lncRNA transcribed opposite the DNMT3L locus (DNMT3L being a catalytically inactive regulator of de novo DNA methyltransferases). No curated functional summary is available; the molecular mechanism and biological role of DNMT3L-AS1 are not well characterized, and any regulatory relationship with DNMT3L is unestablished.

DOP1B.simple: DOP1B makes a protein that has been linked to cognition (thinking and learning) and to early embryonic development, and that is found on the membrane of early endosomes, compartments cells use to sort incoming material. It lies in the chromosome 21 region associated with Down syndrome. Its detailed function is still only partly understood.
DOP1B.technical: DOP1B (DOP1 leucine zipper like protein B) is a protein-coding gene implicated in cognition and embryonic pattern specification, with its product localized to the early endosome membrane, consistent with a role in intracellular membrane trafficking. It maps to the Down syndrome critical region of chromosome 21, though a specific causal mechanism is not firmly established. Its precise molecular function remains incompletely characterized.

DSCAM.simple: DSCAM makes a cell adhesion protein that helps cells recognize and connect with one another, and it plays an important role in the development of the brain and nervous system. It is located in the Down syndrome region of chromosome 21 and is considered a candidate gene for some of the nervous system and heart features associated with Down syndrome. The gene can produce many different protein versions through alternative splicing.
DSCAM.technical: DSCAM (Down syndrome cell adhesion molecule) is a protein-coding member of the immunoglobulin superfamily of cell adhesion molecules (Ig-CAMs) involved in central and peripheral nervous system development. It lies in the Down syndrome region of chromosome 21 and is a candidate contributor to Down syndrome phenotypes including congenital heart disease (DSCHD); a related Ig-CAM gene maps to chromosome 11. Extensive alternative splicing generates multiple isoforms.

DSCAM-AS1.simple: DSCAM-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA, transcribed from the opposite DNA strand of the DSCAM gene. Like other antisense RNAs, it may potentially affect nearby gene activity, but its function is not firmly established. Its specific role has not been well characterized.
DSCAM-AS1.technical: DSCAM-AS1 is an antisense lncRNA transcribed opposite the DSCAM locus on chromosome 21. No curated functional summary is provided here; its molecular mechanism and biological role are not well characterized in this grounding, and any regulatory or disease association should be treated as not established without further evidence.

DSCAM-IT1.simple: DSCAM-IT1 is a non-coding RNA produced from within an intron of the neighboring DSCAM gene, meaning it is read from a region that does not code for protein. Like many such intronic transcripts, its specific role in the cell is not well characterized.
DSCAM-IT1.technical: DSCAM-IT1 is an intronic transcript located within the DSCAM (Down syndrome cell adhesion molecule) gene locus on chromosome 21. It is classified as a non-coding RNA, and its molecular mechanism and function have not been well established. Any regulatory relationship to host-gene DSCAM expression remains to be characterized.

DSCR10.simple: DSCR10 lies in the "Down syndrome critical region" of chromosome 21, a stretch of DNA studied for its links to Down syndrome traits. It is a non-coding RNA gene whose specific function is not well understood.
DSCR10.technical: DSCR10 (Down syndrome critical region 10) is a non-coding RNA gene mapping to the chromosome 21 region historically associated with Down syndrome phenotypes. Its molecular mechanism and function are not well established. Its location in the DSCR has prompted interest, but a defined biological role has not been characterized.

DSCR4.simple: DSCR4 is a non-coding RNA gene in the "Down syndrome critical region" of chromosome 21, an area linked to Down syndrome. It is unusual in being controlled by a two-way (bi-directional) promoter that sits inside an ancient retrovirus sequence embedded in our DNA, and its specific function is not well characterized.
DSCR4.technical: DSCR4 (Down syndrome critical region 4) is located in a chromosome 21 region implicated in the pathogenesis of Down syndrome. It is transcribed from a bi-directional promoter situated within an endogenous retrovirus (ERV), an example of co-option of a transposable-element-derived regulatory sequence. Despite its location of interest, the molecular function of the transcript is not well established.

DSCR8.simple: DSCR8 sits in the "Down syndrome critical region" of chromosome 21, a region studied for its connection to Down syndrome. It is a non-coding RNA gene whose specific role in cells is not well characterized.
DSCR8.technical: DSCR8 (Down syndrome critical region 8) is a non-coding RNA gene within the chromosome 21 Down syndrome critical region. Its molecular mechanism and function have not been well established. Its placement in the DSCR has drawn attention, but no defined biological role is characterized.

DSCR9.simple: DSCR9 is a non-coding RNA gene located in the "Down syndrome critical region" of chromosome 21. Its specific function in the cell is not well understood.
DSCR9.technical: DSCR9 (Down syndrome critical region 9) is a non-coding RNA gene mapping to the chromosome 21 Down syndrome critical region. Its molecular mechanism and function are not well established, and any role beyond its genomic location remains uncharacterized.

ERG.simple: ERG is a protein-coding gene that makes a transcription factor, a protein that switches other genes on or off. It is important for blood vessel formation, blood cell development, and platelet function, and when it gets fused to other genes by chromosome rearrangements it can drive cancers such as prostate cancer, Ewing sarcoma, and certain leukemias.
ERG.technical: ERG encodes a member of the ETS (erythroblast transformation-specific) family of transcription factors, containing an ETS DNA-binding domain and a PNT (pointed) self-association domain. It regulates embryonic development, angiogenesis, hematopoiesis, and megakaryocytic differentiation, and is required for platelet adhesion to the subendothelium and vascular remodeling. ERG is a recurrent fusion partner in oncogenic chromosomal translocations, including TMPRSS2-ERG in prostate cancer, EWS-ERG in Ewing sarcoma, and FUS-ERG in acute myeloid leukemia. Numerous transcript variants arise from three alternative promoters and extensive alternative splicing, though the full-length nature of many has not been determined.

ETS2-AS1.simple: ETS2-AS1 is a non-coding RNA read from the opposite DNA strand of the neighboring ETS2 gene (an "antisense" RNA). Such antisense RNAs can sometimes influence their partner gene, but the specific function of this one is not well characterized.
ETS2-AS1.technical: ETS2-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed from the strand opposite the ETS2 gene on chromosome 21. Antisense lncRNAs can in principle modulate sense-gene expression in cis, but the molecular mechanism and function of ETS2-AS1 have not been well established. Any regulatory relationship to ETS2 remains to be characterized.

EVA1C.simple: EVA1C is a protein-coding gene whose product is found in the space outside cells and can bind heparin, a sulfated sugar molecule. Its broader biological role is only partly understood.
EVA1C.technical: EVA1C (eva-1 homolog C) is a protein-coding gene whose product is reported to enable heparin-binding activity and localize to the extracellular region. Beyond these annotated molecular attributes, its detailed mechanism and physiological function are not well established.

FAM3B.simple: FAM3B is a protein-coding gene that makes a secreted signaling molecule, predicted to act like a cytokine, and it has been linked to insulin secretion. The protein is found outside cells, packaged in tiny vesicles called exosomes.
FAM3B.technical: FAM3B (FAM3 metabolism regulating signaling molecule B) is a protein-coding gene predicted to have cytokine activity and implicated in insulin secretion. The encoded protein localizes to extracellular exosomes. Its precise signaling mechanism is only partially characterized.

FRGCA.simple: FRGCA is a long non-coding RNA gene named for its proposed association with gastric (stomach) cancer and regulation by the FOXM1 transcription factor. Its detailed function is not well characterized.
FRGCA.technical: FRGCA (FOXM1-regulated, gastric cancer associated) is a long non-coding RNA. Its name reflects a reported association with gastric cancer and possible regulation by FOXM1, but a well-established molecular mechanism has not been defined. Functional claims should be treated as preliminary pending further validation.

FTCD-AS1.simple: FTCD-AS1 is a non-coding RNA read from the opposite strand of the neighboring FTCD gene (an "antisense" RNA). Its specific role in the cell is not well characterized.
FTCD-AS1.technical: FTCD-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the FTCD gene on chromosome 21. Antisense lncRNAs may regulate their sense partner in cis, but the molecular mechanism and function of FTCD-AS1 have not been well established.

GATD3.simple: GATD3 is a protein-coding gene whose product is likely a mitochondrial protein, belonging to the same family as the Parkinson's-associated DJ-1 protein. It is overexpressed in the brains of fetuses with Down syndrome.
GATD3.technical: GATD3 (glutamine amidotransferase class 1 domain containing 3) encodes a predicted mitochondrial protein of the DJ-1/PfpI family. The protein is reported to be overexpressed in fetal Down syndrome brain, consistent with its chromosome 21 location, and alternative splicing yields multiple transcript variants. Its precise enzymatic and physiological role remains to be fully characterized.

GATD3A.simple: GATD3A is a protein-coding gene whose product is likely a mitochondrial protein in the same family as the Parkinson's-associated DJ-1 protein. It is overexpressed in the brains of fetuses with Down syndrome.
GATD3A.technical: GATD3A (glutamine amidotransferase class 1 domain containing 3) encodes a predicted mitochondrial protein of the DJ-1/PfpI family and is overexpressed in fetal Down syndrome brain, consistent with its chromosome 21 location. Alternative splicing produces multiple transcript variants. Its precise enzymatic and physiological function is not fully established.

GET1.simple: GET1 is a protein-coding gene whose product helps insert certain "tail-anchored" proteins into the membrane of the endoplasmic reticulum, a key step in protein trafficking. It lies in a chromosome 21 region linked to congenital heart disease in Down syndrome.
GET1.technical: GET1 (guided entry of tail-anchored proteins factor 1) encodes a membrane receptor that promotes insertion of tail-anchored proteins into the endoplasmic reticulum membrane via the GET/TRC pathway. It maps to the chromosome 21 candidate region for congenital heart disease in Down syndrome and lies within a maternally-methylated differentially methylated region (DMR), though its transcription appears biallelic rather than imprinted. Alternative splicing yields multiple transcript variants, and a pseudogene has been defined on chromosome 4.

GET1-SH3BGR.simple: GET1-SH3BGR is a "readthrough" transcript, made when transcription continues from one gene straight into its neighbor, joining two adjacent genes into a single RNA. Such transcripts can sometimes produce fusion proteins combining parts of both genes.
GET1-SH3BGR.technical: GET1-SH3BGR represents naturally occurring readthrough transcription between neighboring genes on chromosome 21 (annotated as WRB/GET1 and SH3BGR). Readthrough transcripts of this locus may encode fusion proteins sharing sequence identity with each individual gene product. The functional significance of the readthrough product is not well established.

GRIK1-AS1.simple: GRIK1-AS1 is a non-coding RNA read from the opposite strand of the GRIK1 glutamate receptor gene (an "antisense" RNA). Its specific function in the cell is not well characterized.
GRIK1-AS1.technical: GRIK1-AS1 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the GRIK1 (glutamate ionotropic receptor kainate type subunit 1) gene on chromosome 21. While antisense lncRNAs can regulate their sense partner in cis, the molecular mechanism and function of GRIK1-AS1 have not been well established.

GRIK1-AS2.simple: GRIK1-AS2 is a second non-coding RNA read from the opposite strand of the GRIK1 glutamate receptor gene (an "antisense" RNA). Its specific role is not well characterized.
GRIK1-AS2.technical: GRIK1-AS2 is a long non-coding antisense RNA transcribed opposite the GRIK1 gene on chromosome 21, distinct from GRIK1-AS1. Its molecular mechanism and function have not been well established, and any regulatory relationship to GRIK1 remains to be characterized.

HEPFAL.simple: HEPFAL is a long non-coding RNA gene named for a proposed association with ferroptosis (a form of iron-dependent cell death) in hepatocellular carcinoma, a type of liver cancer. Its detailed function is not well characterized.
HEPFAL.technical: HEPFAL (hepatocellular carcinoma ferroptosis associative lncRNA) is a long non-coding RNA. Its name reflects a reported association with ferroptosis in hepatocellular carcinoma, but a well-established molecular mechanism has not been defined. Functional and disease claims should be treated as preliminary pending further validation.

HLCS.simple: HLCS is a protein-coding gene that makes an enzyme which attaches the vitamin biotin to other proteins, allowing them to work properly. It is important for making glucose, building fatty acids, and breaking down certain amino acids, and inherited defects in this gene cause holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency, a treatable metabolic disorder.
HLCS.technical: HLCS (holocarboxylase synthetase) encodes the enzyme that covalently attaches biotin to carboxylases and to histones. It is essential for gluconeogenesis, fatty acid synthesis, and branched-chain amino acid catabolism through biotinylation of the biotin-dependent carboxylases. Loss-of-function defects cause holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency, an autosomal recessive multiple carboxylase deficiency. Multiple alternatively spliced variants encoding the same protein have been identified.

HLCS-AS1.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene that sits on the opposite DNA strand from the gene HLCS and is read in the reverse direction (an antisense RNA). It does not make a protein, and its specific role is not well characterized.
HLCS-AS1.technical: HLCS-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed from the strand opposite the HLCS (holocarboxylase synthetase) locus. Antisense lncRNAs can in principle influence expression of their overlapping or neighboring gene, but no specific molecular mechanism or function has been well established for this transcript. Its biological and disease relevance remain uncharacterized.

HSF2BP.simple: This gene makes a protein that binds to and partners with HSF2, a regulator of the cell's heat-shock (stress) response. By interacting with HSF2, it may help fine-tune when that stress-response program is switched on.
HSF2BP.technical: HSF2BP encodes a protein-coding gene (not a non-coding RNA) whose product associates with heat shock transcription factor 2 (HSF2). The interaction occurs between the trimerization domain of HSF2 and the N-terminal hydrophilic region of HSF2BP, which contains two leucine zipper motifs, suggesting a role in modulating HSF2 activation. Established functional and disease context beyond this binding interaction is limited.

IL10RB.simple: This gene makes part of the receptor that lets cells respond to interleukin-10, a signaling molecule that helps calm inflammation. It is an accessory piece needed alongside another subunit to form a working receptor, and it is also shared by several interferon receptors.
IL10RB.technical: IL10RB encodes a protein-coding gene belonging to the class II cytokine receptor family; it is an accessory beta chain essential for the functional interleukin-10 receptor complex. Co-expression with IL10RA is required for IL-10-induced signal transduction, and the subunit is also shared by other class II cytokine receptors. The gene lies within a class II cytokine receptor cluster on chromosome 21 that includes IFNAR2, IFNAR1, and IFNGR2.

IL10RB-DT.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene located next to IL10RB and transcribed in the opposite (divergent) direction from a shared regulatory region. It does not code for a protein, and its specific function is not well characterized.
IL10RB-DT.technical: IL10RB-DT is a divergent transcript (long non-coding RNA) arising from the bidirectional promoter region near IL10RB on chromosome 21. Divergent lncRNAs are often associated with the regulatory activity of their shared promoter, but no specific, well-established molecular function has been demonstrated for this transcript. Its biological and disease significance remain uncharacterized.

ITGB2-AS1.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene read from the opposite DNA strand of the gene ITGB2 (an antisense RNA). It does not make a protein, and its specific role is not well established.
ITGB2-AS1.technical: ITGB2-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed opposite the ITGB2 (integrin beta-2) locus. Antisense lncRNAs can potentially regulate expression of their cognate sense gene, but a specific, well-validated mechanism or function has not been established for this transcript. Its disease relevance remains uncharacterized.

JAM2.simple: This gene makes a junctional adhesion molecule, a protein that helps cells stick together at tight junctions, which act as barriers between cells in tissue layers. A mutation affecting this gene has been linked to a serious brain disorder involving abnormal calcium deposits and cataracts present at birth.
JAM2.technical: JAM2 encodes a protein-coding gene of the immunoglobulin superfamily and the junctional adhesion molecule (JAM) family, localized to tight junctions of high endothelial cells. The protein forms only weak homotypic interactions and acts as a receptor for another JAM family member, with alternative splicing producing multiple transcript variants. A mutation in an intron of this gene is associated with hemorrhagic destruction of the brain, subependymal calcification, and congenital cataracts.

KCNJ6-AS1.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene read from the opposite strand of the gene KCNJ6 (an antisense RNA). It does not code for a protein, and its specific function is not well characterized.
KCNJ6-AS1.technical: KCNJ6-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed opposite the KCNJ6 (a potassium channel gene) locus. While antisense lncRNAs may in principle modulate their overlapping sense gene, no specific molecular mechanism or function has been well established for this transcript. Its biological and disease context remain uncharacterized.

KRTAP10-2.simple: This gene makes one of the keratin-associated proteins, which help build the tough internal structure of hair fibers. It belongs to a family of similar genes clustered together on chromosome 21.
KRTAP10-2.technical: KRTAP10-2 encodes a protein-coding gene of the high-sulfur keratin-associated protein (KAP) family. KAP proteins cross-link with keratin intermediate filaments to form the matrix that contributes to hair fiber structure. The gene resides in a cluster of related KAP genes at 21q22.3, and alternatively spliced transcript variants have been described.
