LINC02943.simple: LINC02943 is a long non-coding RNA gene, copied into RNA that does not code for a protein. Its specific role is not currently known.
LINC02943.technical: LINC02943 is a long intergenic non-coding RNA (lincRNA) located on chromosome 21. There are no established molecular mechanisms, validated targets, or disease associations for this transcript, and its function remains uncharacterized.

LNCSIK1.simple: LNCSIK1 is a long non-coding RNA gene, meaning its RNA product is not translated into a protein. Its name links it to the neighboring SIK1 gene region, but its own specific function has not been clearly established.
LNCSIK1.technical: LNCSIK1 is a long non-coding RNA on chromosome 21, named for its proximity to the SIK1 (salt-inducible kinase 1) locus. Despite the naming, no specific molecular mechanism, regulatory relationship with SIK1, or disease association has been firmly characterized for this transcript, and its function should be regarded as uncharacterized pending experimental data.

LRRC3-DT.simple: LRRC3-DT is a non-coding RNA gene that sits next to the LRRC3 gene and is read off in the opposite direction (a 'divergent transcript'). It does not make a protein, and its specific function is not well established.
LRRC3-DT.technical: LRRC3-DT is a divergent transcript (head-to-head, oppositely oriented non-coding RNA) sharing a bidirectional promoter region with the protein-coding gene LRRC3 on chromosome 21. Divergent lncRNAs of this kind are sometimes associated with regulation of their partner gene's transcription, but no specific mechanism or function has been established for LRRC3-DT, and its biological role remains uncharacterized.

MCM3AP-AS1.simple: MCM3AP-AS1 is an antisense RNA gene, meaning it is read from the opposite DNA strand of the MCM3AP gene and does not make a protein. Antisense RNAs can sometimes influence their partner gene, but the specific, well-established function of this one is limited.
MCM3AP-AS1.technical: MCM3AP-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed from the opposite strand of the MCM3AP locus on chromosome 21. Antisense lncRNAs can in principle regulate the expression of their sense-strand partner gene, though a specific validated mechanism for MCM3AP-AS1 is not firmly established. It has been reported in the cancer literature as a candidate regulatory lncRNA, but such associations should be treated as not yet textbook-established.

MIR10396A.simple: MIR10396A is a microRNA gene. MicroRNAs are very short RNAs that fine-tune which proteins a cell makes by binding to messenger RNAs and reducing their use. The specific targets of this particular microRNA are not well established.
MIR10396A.technical: MIR10396A encodes a microRNA precursor (stem-loop). After processing by Drosha and Dicer, the mature ~22-nt miRNA is loaded into the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC) and guides post-transcriptional repression of target mRNAs through imperfect base-pairing, typically causing translational inhibition or mRNA destabilization. No specific validated targets or disease associations are well established for this particular miRNA.

MIR10396B.simple: MIR10396B is a microRNA gene. MicroRNAs are tiny RNAs that help control gene activity by dampening the production of proteins from specific messenger RNAs. The specific targets of this microRNA are not well characterized.
MIR10396B.technical: MIR10396B encodes a microRNA precursor stem-loop that is processed by the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway into a mature ~22-nt miRNA. The mature strand acts within RISC to repress target mRNAs via imperfect complementarity, leading to translational inhibition or transcript decay. No specific, validated targets or disease links have been established for this miRNA.

MIR125B2.simple: MIR125B2 is one of the genes that produces the microRNA miR-125b, a small RNA that helps regulate gene activity by reducing the output of certain messenger RNAs. miR-125b is broadly involved in cell growth and differentiation, though detailed roles are best described in general terms.
MIR125B2.technical: MIR125B2 is one of two genomic loci encoding the mature miR-125b microRNA (the other being MIR125B1). It is transcribed and processed via the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway into the mature miR-125b, which loads into RISC and post-transcriptionally represses target mRNAs through seed-based imperfect pairing. miR-125b is an evolutionarily conserved miRNA broadly implicated in development, hematopoiesis, and cell proliferation/differentiation; while it has been studied in cancer contexts, specific target claims should be kept general as its outputs are context-dependent.

MIR155.simple: MIR155 is the gene for microRNA miR-155, a small regulatory RNA. miR-155 is especially important in the immune system and in inflammation, and changes in its levels have been linked to certain cancers, particularly lymphomas.
MIR155.technical: MIR155 encodes the miR-155 microRNA precursor, processed by Drosha and Dicer into the mature miR-155 that operates within RISC to post-transcriptionally silence target mRNAs. miR-155 is a well-established regulator of immune cell development, inflammatory responses, and hematopoiesis, and its overexpression is a recognized feature of several B-cell lymphomas and other malignancies, where it can act as an oncogenic miRNA (oncomiR). It is processed from the MIR155HG host-gene transcript (formerly BIC).

MIR155HG.simple: MIR155HG is the host gene that carries the miR-155 microRNA within its longer RNA. This long RNA is expressed at high levels in lymphoma and is thought to act as an oncogene, contributing to cancer development.
MIR155HG.technical: MIR155HG (MIR155 host gene, formerly known as BIC) is a non-coding host-gene transcript from which the oncogenic miR-155 microRNA is processed. The long host transcript itself is expressed at high levels in lymphoma and has been reported to function as an oncogene. As such, MIR155HG is biologically and clinically linked to B-cell lymphomas and broader immune/inflammatory regulation through both the host RNA and its embedded miR-155 product.

MIR3118-1.simple: This gene is the blueprint for a microRNA, a very short RNA molecule that acts as a kind of dimmer switch for other genes. MicroRNAs work by latching onto messenger RNAs and lowering how much protein gets made from them. This particular one is a predicted microRNA, and its specific gene targets and biological role have not been well characterized.
MIR3118-1.technical: MIR3118-1 is one of several genomic copies encoding the MIR3118 microRNA precursor; the RefSeq entry represents the predicted stem-loop (pre-miRNA). Like other miRNAs, the primary transcript is processed by Drosha and Dicer to yield a mature ~22-nt guide strand that loads into RISC and represses target mRNAs through seed-based, typically imperfect pairing. No validated targets or specific physiological or disease functions are well established for this miRNA.

MIR3156-3.simple: This gene produces a microRNA, a tiny RNA that helps fine-tune how strongly other genes are switched on by tagging their messenger RNAs for reduced translation or breakdown. It is a predicted microRNA, so its specific targets and role in the body are not yet established.
MIR3156-3.technical: MIR3156-3 is one of multiple genomic loci for the MIR3156 microRNA family; the RefSeq represents the predicted microRNA stem-loop. The precursor is expected to follow the canonical Drosha/Dicer maturation pathway to produce a mature miRNA that guides RISC to complementary mRNA targets. Validated targets and any specific biological or clinical significance remain uncharacterized.

MIR3197.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that helps regulate other genes by reducing the amount of protein made from their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and function are not well characterized.
MIR3197.technical: MIR3197 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop processed via the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway into a mature ~22-nt guide strand that acts within RISC to repress target mRNAs through seed-mediated pairing. No well-established validated targets, mechanisms, or disease associations are documented for this miRNA.

MIR3648-1.simple: This gene is one of two genomic copies that make a microRNA, a very small RNA that dials down the activity of other genes by acting on their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and biological role have not been clearly established.
MIR3648-1.technical: MIR3648-1 is one of two genomic copies encoding the MIR3648 microRNA precursor; the RefSeq represents the predicted stem-loop. Maturation is expected to proceed through Drosha and Dicer cleavage to generate a mature miRNA that loads into RISC for post-transcriptional silencing of target mRNAs. Specific validated targets and physiological or pathological functions are not well characterized.

MIR3648-2.simple: This gene is a second genomic copy that produces the MIR3648 microRNA, a tiny RNA that helps regulate other genes by limiting protein output from their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and function are not well characterized.
MIR3648-2.technical: MIR3648-2 is a second genomic copy encoding the MIR3648 microRNA precursor; the RefSeq represents the predicted stem-loop. The precursor is expected to undergo canonical Drosha/Dicer processing to yield a mature miRNA that directs RISC-mediated repression of complementary mRNA targets. No validated targets or established biological or disease roles are documented.

MIR4327.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that fine-tunes gene activity by reducing how much protein is made from target messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and role remain uncharacterized.
MIR4327.technical: MIR4327 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop that is expected to be processed by Drosha and Dicer into a mature ~22-nt guide strand acting through RISC to silence target mRNAs via seed-based pairing. Validated targets and any specific biological or clinical context are not well established.

MIR4759.simple: This gene makes a microRNA, a very short RNA that helps control other genes by lowering the amount of protein produced from their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and function are not well characterized.
MIR4759.technical: MIR4759 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop processed via the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway to produce a mature miRNA that loads into RISC and represses target mRNAs through imperfect, seed-mediated pairing. No validated targets or well-established physiological or disease functions are documented for this miRNA.

MIR4760.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a tiny non-coding RNA that acts as a regulator, tuning down other genes by acting on their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and role are not yet established.
MIR4760.technical: MIR4760 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop expected to follow canonical Drosha/Dicer maturation, yielding a mature guide strand that directs RISC-mediated post-transcriptional repression of complementary mRNAs. Specific validated targets, mechanisms, and disease associations remain uncharacterized.

MIR548X.simple: This gene produces a microRNA belonging to the large MIR548 family, a group of short RNAs that help regulate other genes by reducing protein production from their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and function are not well characterized.
MIR548X.technical: MIR548X encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop within the primate-expanded MIR548 family, which is notable for its many genomic copies and sequence diversity. The precursor is expected to be processed by Drosha and Dicer into a mature miRNA acting through RISC to repress target mRNAs. No validated targets or well-established biological or disease roles are documented for this specific member.

MIR548XHG.simple: This gene is the host gene for the MIR548X microRNA, meaning it is a longer transcript that carries the small microRNA within it before that microRNA is cut out and processed. Host genes can themselves act as long non-coding RNAs, but the specific function of this transcript beyond hosting MIR548X is not well characterized.
MIR548XHG.technical: MIR548XHG is the host gene for the MIR548X microRNA; such host transcripts serve as the primary transcript (pri-miRNA) source from which the embedded microRNA is excised by Drosha during processing. Beyond its role in delivering MIR548X, any independent function of the MIR548XHG transcript, including possible long non-coding RNA activity, is not well established. No validated mechanism or disease association is documented.

MIR5692B.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that helps fine-tune gene activity by reducing protein output from target messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and role are not well characterized.
MIR5692B.technical: MIR5692B encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop expected to undergo canonical Drosha/Dicer processing into a mature ~22-nt guide strand that loads into RISC to silence complementary mRNAs through seed-based pairing. Validated targets and any specific biological or clinical significance are not established.

MIR6070.simple: This gene makes a microRNA, a very short RNA that acts as a regulatory switch, dialing down other genes by acting on their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and function have not been established.
MIR6070.technical: MIR6070 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop processed via the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway to produce a mature miRNA acting within RISC to repress target mRNAs. No validated targets or well-established physiological or disease functions are documented.

MIR6130.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a tiny non-coding RNA that helps control other genes by reducing how much protein is made from their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and role are not well characterized.
MIR6130.technical: MIR6130 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop expected to follow canonical Drosha/Dicer maturation into a mature guide strand that directs RISC-mediated post-transcriptional silencing of complementary mRNAs. Specific validated targets, mechanisms, and disease associations remain uncharacterized.

MIR6501.simple: This gene produces a microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that fine-tunes gene activity by lowering protein production from target messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and function are not yet established.
MIR6501.technical: MIR6501 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop processed by Drosha and Dicer into a mature ~22-nt guide strand that acts through RISC to repress target mRNAs via seed-mediated pairing. No validated targets or well-established biological or clinical context are documented.

MIR6508.simple: This gene makes a microRNA, a very short RNA that helps regulate other genes by acting on their messenger RNAs to reduce protein output. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and role are not well characterized.
MIR6508.technical: MIR6508 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop expected to undergo canonical Drosha/Dicer processing into a mature miRNA that loads into RISC for post-transcriptional repression of complementary mRNAs. Validated targets and any specific physiological or disease functions are not established.

MIR663A.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that helps tune the activity of other genes by reducing protein production from their messenger RNAs. MIR663A is one of two related family members and has been studied in various contexts, though for a genome-browser reference its general role is best described as a regulatory microRNA whose validated functions are still being clarified.
MIR663A.technical: MIR663A encodes a microRNA stem-loop processed via the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway into a mature guide strand that acts through RISC to repress target mRNAs by seed-mediated pairing. It belongs to a small family (with MIR663B) and has been examined in cancer and inflammation research literature; however, specific validated targets are best treated cautiously and the full physiological role is not definitively established at textbook level. The RefSeq represents the predicted stem-loop.

MIR6724-1.simple: This gene is one of several genomic copies that make the MIR6724 microRNA, a very short RNA that helps regulate other genes by acting on their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and function are not well characterized.
MIR6724-1.technical: MIR6724-1 is one of multiple genomic copies encoding the MIR6724 microRNA precursor; the RefSeq represents the predicted stem-loop. The precursor is expected to follow canonical Drosha/Dicer processing to yield a mature miRNA acting within RISC to silence target mRNAs. No validated targets or established biological or disease roles are documented.

MIR6724-2.simple: This gene is another genomic copy producing the MIR6724 microRNA, a tiny RNA that helps dial down other genes by acting on their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA whose specific targets and function are not well characterized.
MIR6724-2.technical: MIR6724-2 is one of multiple genomic copies encoding the MIR6724 microRNA precursor; the RefSeq represents the predicted stem-loop. Maturation is expected to proceed through Drosha and Dicer cleavage to generate a mature miRNA that loads into RISC for post-transcriptional silencing. Specific validated targets and physiological or pathological functions are not established.

MIR6724-3.simple: This gene is a further genomic copy that produces the MIR6724 microRNA, a short RNA that helps regulate other genes by reducing protein output from their messenger RNAs. It is a predicted microRNA, and its specific targets and role have not been established.
MIR6724-3.technical: MIR6724-3 is one of multiple genomic copies encoding the MIR6724 microRNA precursor; the RefSeq represents the predicted stem-loop. The precursor is expected to undergo canonical Drosha/Dicer processing to yield a mature miRNA that directs RISC-mediated repression of complementary mRNA targets. No validated targets or well-established biological or disease functions are documented.

MIR6724-4.simple: This gene makes a microRNA, a very short piece of RNA that acts as a fine-tuning switch for other genes. MicroRNAs bind to messenger RNA molecules and reduce how much protein gets made from them. The specific genes this particular microRNA controls have not been well established.
MIR6724-4.technical: MIR6724-4 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop precursor. Following the canonical pathway, the primary transcript is processed by Drosha and Dicer into a mature ~22-nt miRNA that is loaded into RISC and silences target mRNAs through imperfect base pairing, typically causing translational repression or transcript destabilization. This is one of several genomic copies annotated for miR-6724; its validated targets and physiological roles are not well characterized.

MIR6814.simple: This gene produces a microRNA, a tiny RNA molecule that helps regulate the activity of other genes by dampening the production of their proteins. MicroRNAs work by latching onto messenger RNAs and blocking or degrading them. The particular targets and function of this microRNA are not well established.
MIR6814.technical: MIR6814 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop. Its mature product is expected to be generated by the standard Drosha/Dicer processing route and incorporated into RISC, where it would guide post-transcriptional silencing of target mRNAs via imperfect complementarity. No specific validated targets or biological functions are well characterized for this miRNA.

MIR6815.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that fine-tunes gene expression by reducing the output of target genes. MicroRNAs do this by pairing with messenger RNAs and promoting their silencing. The specific targets of this microRNA are not well established.
MIR6815.technical: MIR6815 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop precursor processed by the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway into a mature miRNA that associates with RISC to silence complementary target mRNAs. As with most newly annotated miRNAs, its specific targets, expression pattern, and biological role remain uncharacterized.

MIR802.simple: This gene makes a microRNA, a small regulatory RNA that tunes down the activity of other genes by binding their messenger RNAs. MicroRNAs are important for controlling many cellular processes. The precise targets and role of this particular microRNA are not firmly established in humans.
MIR802.technical: MIR802 encodes a microRNA stem-loop processed via the canonical Drosha/Dicer pathway, yielding a mature miRNA that operates through RISC-mediated post-transcriptional silencing of target mRNAs. miR-802 lies within the Down syndrome critical region on chromosome 21 and has been studied as a candidate dosage-sensitive miRNA; however, its specific validated human targets and disease contributions remain under investigation rather than firmly established.

MIR8069.simple: This gene produces a microRNA, a tiny RNA that helps control how strongly other genes are expressed by interfering with their messenger RNAs. The specific genes it regulates and its biological role are not well established.
MIR8069.technical: MIR8069 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop. Its mature miRNA is expected to be generated by Drosha/Dicer processing and to act through RISC to silence target mRNAs via imperfect base pairing. Validated targets and functional roles for this miRNA are not well characterized.

MIR8069-1.simple: This gene encodes a microRNA, a short regulatory RNA that dampens the expression of target genes by binding their messenger RNAs. It is one of multiple genomic copies annotated for mir-8069. The specific targets and function are not well established.
MIR8069-1.technical: MIR8069-1 is a predicted microRNA stem-loop locus, one of several genomic copies annotated for mir-8069. The mature product would follow the canonical Drosha/Dicer biogenesis route and act within RISC to mediate post-transcriptional silencing of complementary mRNAs. No specific validated targets or functions are established.

MIR8069-2.simple: This gene makes a microRNA, a small non-coding RNA that fine-tunes gene activity by reducing protein output from target messenger RNAs. It is another genomic copy of mir-8069. Its specific targets and role are not well established.
MIR8069-2.technical: MIR8069-2 encodes a predicted microRNA stem-loop and represents an additional genomic copy of mir-8069. Its mature miRNA would be produced by standard Drosha/Dicer processing and loaded into RISC to silence target mRNAs through imperfect complementarity. Specific validated targets and biological functions remain uncharacterized.

MIR99A.simple: This gene produces miR-99a, a microRNA that is part of a well-known, evolutionarily conserved family of regulatory RNAs. Like other microRNAs, it tunes down the activity of target genes by binding their messenger RNAs, and it belongs to a cluster that also includes let-7c. It is involved in normal development and cell growth control.
MIR99A.technical: MIR99A encodes the microRNA miR-99a, a conserved member of the miR-99/100 family, and is co-transcribed within the mir-99a-let-7c cluster hosted by MIR99AHG on chromosome 21. The mature miRNA is generated by canonical Drosha/Dicer processing and acts via RISC to repress target mRNAs through seed-based imperfect pairing. miR-99a is generally associated with regulation of cell proliferation and growth-signaling pathways; while broadly studied in development and cancer contexts, claims of specific individual targets should be treated as functional context rather than universally established.

MIR99AHG.simple: This is a host gene whose transcript carries the mir-99a-let-7c microRNA cluster, meaning it serves as the source RNA from which several microRNAs are processed. It is predicted to contribute to microRNA-based gene silencing. Beyond hosting these microRNAs, its own independent function is not well established.
MIR99AHG.technical: MIR99AHG is a long non-coding RNA gene that acts as the host transcript for the mir-99a-let-7c microRNA cluster (including MIR99A, MIRLET7C, and miR-125b family members). It is predicted to be involved in miRNA-mediated post-transcriptional gene silencing and to be associated with the RISC complex, primarily reflecting its role as the precursor host for the embedded miRNAs. Whether the spliced lncRNA itself has additional sequence-specific regulatory functions independent of its hosted miRNAs is not well characterized.

MIRLET7C.simple: This gene encodes let-7c, a member of the famous let-7 family of microRNAs that was among the first microRNAs ever discovered. let-7 microRNAs are highly conserved across animals and act as important regulators of cell growth and development, often functioning to keep cell proliferation in check. Like all microRNAs, it works by binding messenger RNAs and reducing their protein output.
MIRLET7C.technical: MIRLET7C encodes let-7c, a member of the deeply conserved let-7 microRNA family, and is part of the mir-99a-let-7c cluster on chromosome 21. The mature miRNA is generated by Drosha/Dicer processing and silences targets via RISC and seed-region complementarity. The let-7 family is well established as a regulator of developmental timing and is broadly characterized as tumor-suppressive through repression of proliferative and stemness programs; specific individual targets should be framed as family-level context rather than asserting validated targets for this particular paralog.

MIS18A.simple: This gene does not make a non-coding RNA; it encodes the MIS18A protein, which helps cells correctly assemble their centromeres, the structures that allow chromosomes to be evenly distributed when a cell divides. Accurate chromosome distribution is essential for healthy cell division.
MIS18A.technical: MIS18A is a protein-coding gene, not a non-coding RNA. The encoded protein is a component of the Mis18 complex that licenses centromeres for new CENP-A (centromeric histone H3 variant) deposition, and it is predicted to be involved in CENP-A-containing chromatin assembly, chromosome segregation, and pericentric heterochromatin organization. The protein localizes to the nucleoplasm and cytosol and functions through identical protein binding to support proper centromere identity and faithful mitotic chromosome segregation.

MIS18A-AS1.simple: This gene produces a long non-coding RNA that is read from the opposite DNA strand of the neighboring MIS18A gene. Such antisense RNAs sometimes help regulate their partner gene, but the specific role of this one has not been established.
MIS18A-AS1.technical: MIS18A-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed from the strand opposite MIS18A. No curated functional summary is available, and like many antisense lncRNAs its mechanism is uncertain; it may potentially influence expression of the overlapping MIS18A locus in cis, but its specific function and any role in cis- or trans-regulation are not well characterized.

N6AMT1.simple: This gene does not make a non-coding RNA; it encodes a methyltransferase enzyme, a protein that adds chemical methyl tags to other molecules. This enzyme helps modify a protein involved in stopping protein synthesis correctly, and it also helps convert a toxic arsenic byproduct into a less harmful form. A related non-functional copy (pseudogene) exists on chromosome 11.
N6AMT1.technical: N6AMT1 is a protein-coding gene encoding an N(6)-adenine-specific methyltransferase (HemK methyltransferase 2). The enzyme is implicated in methylation of the translation termination release factor (ETF1/eRF1) and in the methylation of histone H4 lysine and glutamine residues, and it participates in arsenic biotransformation by converting monomethylarsonous acid (MMA-III) to the less toxic dimethylarsonic acid. Alternative splicing yields multiple transcript variants, and a related pseudogene is annotated on chromosome 11.

NCOR1P4.simple: This is a pseudogene, a copy of the NCOR1 gene that has lost the ability to make a working protein. Pseudogenes are generally not expected to produce a functional product, and no specific function has been established for this one.
NCOR1P4.technical: NCOR1P4 is annotated as pseudogene 4 of NCOR1 (nuclear receptor corepressor 1). As a pseudogene it is not expected to encode a functional protein, and no curated functional summary or established regulatory role is available. Any potential function (e.g., as a regulatory transcript) would be speculative and is not characterized.

PAXBP1.simple: This gene does not make a non-coding RNA; it encodes a protein that binds to the PAX3 and PAX7 transcription factors. It is predicted to help these factors regulate gene activity, with a particular role in muscle stem cell and myoblast proliferation. This makes it relevant to muscle development.
PAXBP1.technical: PAXBP1 is a protein-coding gene encoding a PAX3/PAX7-binding adaptor protein. It is predicted to enable DNA binding and histone methyltransferase binding and to participate in positive regulation of RNA polymerase II-dependent transcription, acting upstream of or within the regulation of myoblast and skeletal muscle satellite cell proliferation. The protein has been localized to the cytosol and functions in the context of PAX-driven transcriptional programs in muscle progenitor cells.

PAXBP1-AS1.simple: This gene produces a long non-coding RNA read from the opposite DNA strand of the PAXBP1 gene. Antisense RNAs like this can sometimes regulate their neighboring gene, but the specific function of this one has not been established.
PAXBP1-AS1.technical: PAXBP1-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed opposite the PAXBP1 locus. No curated functional summary is available; as with many antisense lncRNAs, its mechanism is uncertain and any potential cis-regulatory effect on PAXBP1 remains unestablished and not well characterized.

PCBP3.simple: This gene does not make a non-coding RNA; it encodes poly(rC) binding protein 3, a protein that grabs onto RNA at stretches rich in the base cytosine. Such proteins help control the stability and processing of messenger RNAs after they are made. Different versions of the protein are produced by alternative splicing.
PCBP3.technical: PCBP3 is a protein-coding gene encoding a member of the KH-domain alpha-CP (poly(rC)-binding protein) subfamily that binds C-rich pyrimidine RNA elements and functions in post-transcriptional regulation. Unlike some other alpha-CP family members, the PCBP3 protein lacks canonical nuclear localization signals, suggesting distinct subcellular distribution. Alternative splicing produces multiple transcript variants encoding distinct isoforms.

PCBP3-AS1.simple: This gene produces a long non-coding RNA read from the strand opposite the PCBP3 gene. Such antisense RNAs may help regulate their partner gene, but the specific function of this transcript has not been established.
PCBP3-AS1.technical: PCBP3-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA transcribed opposite PCBP3. No curated functional summary is available; its mechanism and any cis-regulatory relationship to PCBP3 are uncertain and not well characterized, consistent with the limited annotation of most antisense lncRNAs.

PCSEAT.simple: This gene produces a long non-coding RNA whose name reflects that it is expressed in prostate cancer and associates with the chromatin regulator EZH2. Its detailed function is not well established, though its name points to a possible link with prostate cancer biology.
PCSEAT.technical: PCSEAT (prostate cancer expressed EZH2-associated transcript) is a long non-coding RNA named for its reported expression in prostate cancer and association with EZH2, the catalytic subunit of the Polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2). No curated functional summary is provided here, and while the name implies a potential role in PRC2/EZH2-related epigenetic regulation in prostate cancer, the specific mechanism is not well established and should be regarded as a candidate context rather than a validated function.

PDE9A-AS1.simple: PDE9A-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA gene, meaning it is copied into RNA but does not carry instructions for making a protein. It sits on the opposite DNA strand from the PDE9A gene and runs in the reverse direction, which is why it is called an antisense RNA. Its specific function is not well understood; antisense RNAs like this can sometimes help fine-tune the activity of their neighboring gene, but for this one no clear role has been established.
PDE9A-AS1.technical: PDE9A-AS1 is an antisense long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed from the strand opposite the PDE9A locus. As with many natural antisense transcripts, it could in principle modulate expression of PDE9A in cis through transcriptional interference, chromatin effects, or RNA duplex formation, but no specific molecular mechanism has been validated. There is no well-established functional or disease association for this transcript, and its role should be regarded as uncharacterized.

PICSAR.simple: PICSAR is a long non-coding RNA gene, a stretch of DNA that is copied into RNA but does not make a protein. It was identified in connection with cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, a common skin cancer, and its name reflects that this RNA tends to be elevated in those tumor cells. While it has been studied as a possible promoter of cancer cell growth, the precise details of how it acts are still being worked out.
PICSAR.technical: PICSAR (P38-inhibited cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma associated lincRNA) is a long intergenic non-coding RNA (lincRNA) originally described in the context of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), where it is reported to be upregulated. It has been proposed to promote tumor cell proliferation and migration, with its expression influenced by p38 MAPK signaling. Beyond this cancer-associated context, the detailed molecular mechanism is not definitively established, so specific downstream targets should be interpreted with caution.

PLAC4.simple: PLAC4 is a gene expressed mainly in the placenta and is transcribed into RNA without producing a well-defined protein product. Its main practical interest has been as a placenta-specific RNA detectable in maternal blood, which has been explored for non-invasive prenatal testing. Its precise biological function is not well characterized.
PLAC4.technical: PLAC4 (placenta associated 4) is a placentally expressed transcript located on chromosome 21 that is predicted to be associated with the plasma membrane. It is best known not for a defined protein function but as a placenta-derived RNA studied for non-invasive prenatal assessment of fetal chromosome 21 status. Its intrinsic molecular and cellular role remains poorly characterized.

POFUT2.simple: POFUT2 is a protein-coding gene; despite being grouped here, it makes an actual enzyme rather than a non-coding RNA. The enzyme attaches a sugar called fucose directly onto certain proteins, specifically onto serine or threonine residues within a structural motif called thrombospondin type-1 repeats. This sugar modification helps such proteins fold and function correctly.
POFUT2.technical: POFUT2 (protein O-fucosyltransferase 2) is a protein-coding gene encoding an O-fucosyltransferase. The enzyme transfers fucose in direct O-linkage to serine or threonine residues within thrombospondin (THBS) type-1 repeats, using these repeats as substrates. This O-fucosylation contributes to the proper folding and quality control of secreted and membrane proteins bearing such repeats; it is mechanistically distinct from EGF-repeat O-fucosylation carried out by POFUT1.

PPP1R2P2.simple: PPP1R2P2 is a pseudogene, a DNA sequence that resembles the working gene PPP1R2 (an inhibitor of a protein phosphatase) but has lost the ability to produce a functional protein. Pseudogenes are essentially genetic relics. This one is not expected to make a functional product, and no specific role has been established for it.
PPP1R2P2.technical: PPP1R2P2 is a processed/duplicated pseudogene of PPP1R2 (protein phosphatase 1 regulatory inhibitor subunit 2). As a pseudogene it is not expected to encode a functional protein. Although some pseudogenes can be transcribed and exert regulatory effects (for example as competing endogenous RNAs), no such well-established function is documented for PPP1R2P2, and it should be treated as having no validated role.

PRDM15.simple: PRDM15 is a protein-coding gene; despite appearing in this RNA list, it produces a real protein rather than a non-coding RNA. The protein is a transcription factor, meaning it binds DNA and helps turn other genes on or off. It is thought to help regulate signaling pathways and the division of stem cells.
PRDM15.technical: PRDM15 (PR/SET domain 15) is a protein-coding gene encoding a zinc-finger transcriptional regulator of the PRDM family. It is predicted to act as an RNA polymerase II-specific DNA-binding transcription activator with sequence-specific cis-regulatory and promoter chromatin binding activity, and localizes to nuclear bodies. It has been implicated in positive regulation of RNA polymerase II transcription, regulation of signal transduction, and regulation of stem cell division.

PSMG1.simple: PSMG1 is a protein-coding gene; despite its placement here, it makes a functional protein rather than a non-coding RNA. The protein acts as a chaperone that helps assemble the proteasome, the cell's machinery for breaking down and recycling unwanted proteins. By guiding correct assembly, it supports normal protein turnover in the cell.
PSMG1.technical: PSMG1 (proteasome assembly chaperone 1) is a protein-coding gene whose product functions as a molecular adaptor/chaperone in proteasome 20S core particle assembly. It participates in chaperone-mediated protein complex assembly as part of a protein folding chaperone complex, and localizes to several compartments including the endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, and nucleoplasm. Its role is in the biogenesis of the proteasome rather than in any non-coding RNA function.

PTTG1IP.simple: PTTG1IP is a protein-coding gene; it makes a membrane protein rather than a non-coding RNA. The protein binds to PTTG1 (a securin involved in cell division) and helps move it into the cell nucleus. Together they can switch on production of a growth factor that promotes blood vessel formation.
PTTG1IP.technical: PTTG1IP (PTTG1 interacting protein) is a protein-coding gene encoding a single-pass type I integral membrane protein. It binds pituitary tumor-transforming 1 (PTTG1, securin) and facilitates its nuclear translocation; coexpression of PTTG1IP and PTTG1 induces transcriptional activation of basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF). Alternatively spliced transcript variants have been described. This is a protein-coding locus, not a non-coding RNA gene.

RBM11.simple: RBM11 is a protein-coding gene; despite its inclusion here, it encodes a protein rather than a non-coding RNA. The protein binds RNA and helps regulate alternative splicing, the process that lets a single gene produce different versions of a message. It has been linked to the cell's response to oxidative stress.
RBM11.technical: RBM11 (RNA binding motif protein 11) is a protein-coding gene encoding an RNA-binding protein with poly(U) RNA binding and homodimerization activity that localizes to nuclear speckles. It acts in the regulation of alternative mRNA splicing via the spliceosome and has been implicated in the cellular response to oxidative stress. As a splicing regulator it influences RNA processing but is itself a protein, not a non-coding transcript.

RNA18SN1.simple: RNA18SN1 is one of the genes for 18S ribosomal RNA, a core building block of the ribosome, the molecular machine that reads genetic messages and builds proteins. These genes occur in many repeated copies clustered on a few human chromosomes, and this copy is one of those on chromosome 21. The 18S rRNA is an essential, ancient component found in every cell.
RNA18SN1.technical: RNA18SN1 represents a copy of the 18S ribosomal RNA, a structural and catalytic RNA component of the small (40S) ribosomal subunit. It is derived from the 45S rDNA repeat unit transcribed by RNA polymerase I, which is processed into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. These rDNA repeats reside in arrays (RNR1-RNR5) on the acrocentric chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, and 22, with high and variable copy number; this gene is a representative 18S copy on chromosome 21.

RNA18SN2.simple: RNA18SN2 is a copy of the gene for 18S ribosomal RNA, an essential part of the ribosome that cells use to manufacture proteins. Human ribosomal RNA genes exist as many repeated copies grouped on a handful of chromosomes, and this one is among those on chromosome 21. The 18S rRNA is a fundamental, universally conserved component of the protein-making machinery.
RNA18SN2.technical: RNA18SN2 is a representative copy of the 18S rRNA on chromosome 21, part of the small (40S) ribosomal subunit. It arises from processing of the 45S pre-rRNA precursor transcribed by RNA polymerase I, which yields the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. The encoding 45S rDNA repeats are organized in tandem arrays on the acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22) with substantial inter-individual copy-number variation.

RNA18SN3.simple: RNA18SN3 is another copy of the 18S ribosomal RNA gene, encoding a key piece of the ribosome that assembles proteins. These genes are present in numerous repeated copies on several human chromosomes, and this copy lies on chromosome 21. The 18S rRNA itself is an essential and highly conserved molecule found in all cells.
RNA18SN3.technical: RNA18SN3 represents a copy of the 18S rRNA on chromosome 21, a component of the small (40S) ribosomal subunit. It is generated by RNA polymerase I transcription of the 45S rDNA repeat and subsequent processing into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. The rDNA repeats are clustered in the nucleolus organizer regions of the five acrocentric chromosomes, with copy number varying widely among individuals.

RNA18SN4.simple: RNA18SN4 is a copy of the 18S ribosomal RNA gene, which encodes a core part of the ribosome, the cell's protein-building machine. Human ribosomal RNA genes occur as many repeated copies on a few chromosomes; this copy is located on chromosome 22. The 18S rRNA is an essential, evolutionarily ancient molecule.
RNA18SN4.technical: RNA18SN4 is a representative copy of the 18S rRNA, in this case localized to chromosome 22, and forms part of the small (40S) ribosomal subunit. It derives from the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S pre-rRNA, which is processed into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. The underlying 45S rDNA repeats are arrayed on the acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22) with high, variable copy number.

RNA18SN5.simple: RNA18SN5 is a copy of the 18S ribosomal RNA gene, encoding an essential component of the ribosome used to make proteins. It is one of many repeated ribosomal RNA gene copies in the human genome; the exact chromosome for this particular representative copy is not assigned. The 18S rRNA is a fundamental and universally conserved part of the cell's machinery.
RNA18SN5.technical: RNA18SN5 is a representative copy of the 18S rRNA whose chromosomal location is not defined, and it corresponds to a component of the small (40S) ribosomal subunit. It is produced by processing of the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S pre-rRNA into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. These rDNA repeat units occur in tandem arrays on the acrocentric chromosomes with extensive copy-number variation.

RNA28SN1.simple: RNA28SN1 is one of the genes for 28S ribosomal RNA, the largest RNA component of the ribosome, the machine cells use to build proteins. These genes exist in many repeated copies clustered on certain chromosomes, and this copy is on chromosome 21. The 28S rRNA is an essential and highly conserved part of the protein-synthesis apparatus.
RNA28SN1.technical: RNA28SN1 represents a copy of the 28S rRNA on chromosome 21, the principal structural and catalytic RNA of the large (60S) ribosomal subunit. It is generated from the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S pre-rRNA, which is processed into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. The 45S rDNA repeats reside in tandem arrays (RNR1-RNR5) on the acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22), with copy number varying among individuals and chromosomes.

RNA28SN2.simple: RNA28SN2 is a copy of the gene for 28S ribosomal RNA, the largest RNA piece of the ribosome that assembles proteins. The human genome carries many repeated copies of these genes on a few chromosomes, and this one is on chromosome 21. The 28S rRNA is an essential, evolutionarily conserved molecule.
RNA28SN2.technical: RNA28SN2 is a representative copy of the 28S rRNA on chromosome 21 and forms part of the large (60S) ribosomal subunit. It arises from processing of the 45S pre-rRNA transcribed by RNA polymerase I into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. The encoding 45S rDNA repeats are clustered in the nucleolus organizer regions of the acrocentric chromosomes with high, variable copy number.

RNA28SN3.simple: RNA28SN3 is another copy of the 28S ribosomal RNA gene, encoding a major part of the ribosome, the cell's protein factory. These ribosomal RNA genes are present in numerous repeated copies on several chromosomes, and this copy is on chromosome 21. The 28S rRNA is a core, highly conserved component of all cells.
RNA28SN3.technical: RNA28SN3 represents a copy of the 28S rRNA on chromosome 21, a component of the large (60S) ribosomal subunit. It is produced by RNA polymerase I transcription of the 45S rDNA repeat and processing into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. The rDNA repeats are organized in tandem arrays on the five acrocentric chromosomes, with copy number differing among individuals.

RNA28SN5.simple: RNA28SN5 is a copy of the 28S ribosomal RNA gene, which encodes a large, essential part of the ribosome used to build proteins. It is one of many repeated ribosomal RNA gene copies in the genome, and the exact chromosome for this representative copy is not assigned. The 28S rRNA is a fundamental, universally conserved molecule.
RNA28SN5.technical: RNA28SN5 is a representative copy of the 28S rRNA whose chromosomal location is unknown, corresponding to a component of the large (60S) ribosomal subunit. It is derived from the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S pre-rRNA, processed into the 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs. These 45S rDNA repeat units occur in tandem arrays on the acrocentric chromosomes with extensive copy-number variation.

RNA45SN1.simple: RNA45SN1 is a copy of the gene for the 45S pre-ribosomal RNA, the single long precursor molecule from which the cell carves out three of its ribosomal RNAs (18S, 5.8S, and 28S). These genes occur in many repeated copies on a few chromosomes, and this copy is on chromosome 21. This precursor is the starting material for building ribosomes, the machines that make proteins.
RNA45SN1.technical: RNA45SN1 represents a copy of the 45S pre-ribosomal RNA on chromosome 21, the primary RNA polymerase I transcript of the rDNA repeat unit. The 45S precursor is co- and post-transcriptionally processed in the nucleolus into the mature 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNAs that build the small and large ribosomal subunits. The 45S rDNA repeats are arrayed (RNR1-RNR5) on the acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22), with diploid copy number varying widely among individuals.

RNA45SN2.simple: This is one copy of the gene for the 45S ribosomal RNA, a large RNA that cells need to build their protein factories called ribosomes. The 45S RNA is a long precursor that gets cut up to make three of the core RNA pieces of the ribosome. Many copies of this gene exist clustered on a few human chromosomes, including chromosome 21, and they are among the most heavily used genes in the cell.
RNA45SN2.technical: RNA45SN2 is one representative copy of the 45S pre-ribosomal RNA gene, located within the rDNA repeat array on chromosome 21 (one of the NOR-bearing acrocentric chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21 and 22). The 45S precursor is transcribed by RNA polymerase I and processed in the nucleolus to yield the mature 18S, 5.8S and 28S rRNAs, which are structural and catalytic components of the ribosome. rDNA repeat copy number is highly variable between individuals and chromosomes (diploid estimates roughly 60 to >800 units), and these arrays organize the nucleolus; rDNA instability and altered rRNA biogenesis are broadly linked to cellular stress and disease, though this specific copy is not individually characterized.

RNA45SN3.simple: This is another copy of the gene for the 45S ribosomal RNA, the large precursor RNA that cells process to build the core of their ribosomes (the machines that make proteins). The precursor is cut into three of the ribosome's key RNA components. Such genes are present in many copies clustered on chromosome 21 and a few other human chromosomes.
RNA45SN3.technical: RNA45SN3 is a representative copy of the 45S pre-ribosomal RNA gene within the chromosome 21 rDNA repeat array, part of the nucleolar organizer regions on the acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22). Transcribed by RNA polymerase I, the 45S precursor is processed in the nucleolus into the mature 18S, 5.8S and 28S rRNAs. Copy number across the diploid genome is highly variable (roughly 60 to >800 repeat units); this particular copy is not individually functionally characterized beyond its role in ribosome biogenesis.

RNA45SN5.simple: This is one more copy of the 45S ribosomal RNA gene, which makes the large precursor RNA that cells process to build their ribosomes. The precursor is cut into three core RNA pieces of the ribosome. These genes occur in many clustered copies on several human chromosomes.
RNA45SN5.technical: RNA45SN5 is a representative copy of the 45S pre-ribosomal RNA gene; its exact chromosomal location among the acrocentric rDNA arrays (chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, 22) is reported as unknown. The RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S precursor is processed in the nucleolus to the mature 18S, 5.8S and 28S rRNAs. rDNA copy number is highly variable between individuals (diploid estimates ~60 to >800 repeat units), and this specific copy is not individually characterized beyond its canonical role in producing ribosomal RNA.

RNA5-8SN1.simple: This gene encodes the 5.8S ribosomal RNA, one of the small RNA pieces that forms part of the ribosome, the cell's protein-making machine. It is produced as part of a larger 45S precursor RNA that gets cut into several mature pieces. Many copies of this gene sit in clusters on chromosome 21 and a few other human chromosomes.
RNA5-8SN1.technical: RNA5-8SN1 is a representative copy of the 5.8S rRNA on chromosome 21, embedded in the 45S rDNA repeat unit of the acrocentric nucleolar organizer regions. The 5.8S rRNA is excised during RNA polymerase I-dependent processing of the 45S precursor and, together with 28S and 5S rRNAs, forms part of the large (60S) ribosomal subunit, where it contributes to translational structure and function. rDNA copy number is highly variable across individuals (diploid ~60 to >800 units); this individual copy is not separately functionally annotated.

RNA5-8SN2.simple: This gene encodes the 5.8S ribosomal RNA, a small structural RNA that is part of the ribosome, the machine that builds proteins. It is generated by cutting up a larger 45S precursor RNA. Many copies of this gene are clustered on chromosome 21 and a few other human chromosomes.
RNA5-8SN2.technical: RNA5-8SN2 is a representative copy of the 5.8S rRNA on chromosome 21, within the 45S rDNA repeat array of the acrocentric chromosomes. The 5.8S rRNA is produced by nucleolar processing of the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S precursor and is incorporated into the large ribosomal subunit. Repeat copy number varies widely between individuals (diploid ~60 to >800 units); this specific copy has no distinct functional annotation beyond its role in the ribosome.

RNA5-8SN3.simple: This gene encodes the 5.8S ribosomal RNA, a small RNA component of the ribosome, the cell's protein-building machine. It comes from a larger 45S precursor RNA that is cut into several finished pieces. The gene exists in many clustered copies on chromosome 21 and a few other chromosomes.
RNA5-8SN3.technical: RNA5-8SN3 is a representative copy of the 5.8S rRNA on chromosome 21, part of the 45S rDNA repeat unit in the acrocentric nucleolar organizer regions. The 5.8S rRNA is excised from the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S precursor during nucleolar processing and forms part of the large (60S) ribosomal subunit. rDNA copy number is highly variable across the population (diploid ~60 to >800 units); this individual copy is not separately characterized.

RNA5-8SN4.simple: This gene encodes the 5.8S ribosomal RNA, one of the small structural RNAs that make up the ribosome, the machine cells use to build proteins. It is produced by cutting a larger 45S precursor RNA into its finished pieces. Many copies of this gene cluster on chromosome 22 and several other human chromosomes.
RNA5-8SN4.technical: RNA5-8SN4 is a representative copy of the 5.8S rRNA on chromosome 22, within the 45S rDNA repeat array of the acrocentric chromosomes. The 5.8S rRNA arises from nucleolar processing of the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S precursor and is a component of the large ribosomal subunit. rDNA repeat copy number varies widely between individuals (diploid ~60 to >800 units); this specific copy is not individually functionally annotated.

RNA5-8SN5.simple: This gene encodes the 5.8S ribosomal RNA, a small RNA building block of the ribosome, the cell's protein-making machine. It is generated from a larger 45S precursor RNA. The gene occurs in many clustered copies across several human chromosomes.
RNA5-8SN5.technical: RNA5-8SN5 is a representative copy of the 5.8S rRNA whose chromosomal location is reported as unknown among the acrocentric rDNA arrays (chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, 22). The 5.8S rRNA is excised from the RNA polymerase I-transcribed 45S precursor during nucleolar processing and is incorporated into the large ribosomal subunit. rDNA copy number is highly variable between individuals (diploid ~60 to >800 units); this individual copy has no distinct functional annotation.

RSPH1-DT.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene that sits next to the gene RSPH1 and is read off the DNA in the opposite, or divergent, direction from it. Genes like this often do not make proteins, and the specific job of this RNA is not well understood. Such divergent transcripts are sometimes involved in regulating their neighboring gene, but that is not established here.
RSPH1-DT.technical: RSPH1-DT (RSPH1 divergent transcript) is a long non-coding RNA gene transcribed in the opposite orientation from a shared or nearby promoter region with the protein-coding gene RSPH1. No curated functional summary is provided, and its specific molecular role is not well characterized. While divergent lncRNAs can in some cases participate in cis-regulation of the adjacent gene's expression or local chromatin state, no such function is established for this transcript and it should be treated as functionally uncharacterized.

RUNX1-IT1.simple: This is a non-coding RNA produced from within the boundaries of the RUNX1 gene, specifically from one of its intron regions. It does not make a protein, and its precise function is not well understood. Intronic transcripts like this are often poorly characterized.
RUNX1-IT1.technical: RUNX1-IT1 (RUNX1 intronic transcript 1) is a non-coding RNA gene located within an intron of the RUNX1 locus, a major hematopoietic transcription-factor gene on chromosome 21. No curated functional summary is provided, and the specific molecular mechanism or function of this intronic transcript is not well established. Its genomic association with RUNX1 is notable given RUNX1's role in hematopoiesis and leukemia, but a functional or regulatory link for this transcript is not established and it should be regarded as uncharacterized.

SAMSN1-AS1.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene that lies opposite the SAMSN1 gene and is read from the other DNA strand (antisense). It does not make a protein, and its specific function is not well understood. Antisense RNAs sometimes help regulate the gene they overlap, but that is not established here.
SAMSN1-AS1.technical: SAMSN1-AS1 (SAMSN1 antisense RNA 1) is a long non-coding RNA gene transcribed from the strand opposite the protein-coding gene SAMSN1. No curated functional summary is provided, and its specific molecular role is not well characterized. Antisense lncRNAs can in principle modulate the expression, stability, or chromatin context of their sense-strand counterpart, but no such mechanism is established for this transcript, which should be treated as functionally uncharacterized.

SETD4.simple: SETD4 is a protein-coding gene (not a non-coding RNA) that makes an enzyme adding methyl chemical tags onto histones, the proteins that DNA wraps around. By modifying histones it can help control which genes are turned on or off. It has been linked to processes such as inflammation, cell growth, and activity in the bone marrow.
SETD4.technical: SETD4 is a protein-coding gene encoding a SET-domain-containing methyltransferase with reported histone H4K20 methyltransferase activity. It is predicted to be involved in several processes including positive regulation of the inflammatory response, positive regulation of macromolecule biosynthesis, and regulation of cell proliferation in the bone marrow, and the protein localizes to the cytosol and nucleus. As a chromatin-modifying enzyme it can influence gene-expression programs, though several of its annotations are predicted rather than experimentally confirmed.

SMIM34.simple: SMIM34 is a protein-coding gene (not a non-coding RNA) for a small protein that is predicted to sit within cell membranes. Beyond its likely membrane location, its specific function is not well understood.
SMIM34.technical: SMIM34 (small integral membrane protein 34) is a protein-coding gene encoding a small predicted integral membrane protein. The only available annotation is a predicted membrane localization; no specific molecular function, pathway, or disease association is established. It should be regarded as a poorly characterized small membrane protein.

SNORA80A.simple: This is a small nucleolar RNA, a short non-coding RNA that works in the cell's nucleolus to help chemically modify other RNAs, especially ribosomal RNA, as ribosomes are built. As an H/ACA box snoRNA, its general job is to guide a specific modification of target RNA bases. The exact targets of this particular snoRNA are not detailed here.
SNORA80A.technical: SNORA80A is an H/ACA box small nucleolar RNA, predicted to function in RNA processing and to localize to the nucleolus. H/ACA box snoRNAs canonically act as guide RNAs that direct site-specific pseudouridylation of target RNAs (typically rRNA and snRNA) via base-pairing within the H/ACA ribonucleoprotein complex. The specific RNA target sites for this snoRNA are not provided here, so the general class function should be assumed rather than a particular validated target.

SNORA91.simple: This is a small nucleolar RNA, a short non-coding RNA that operates in the nucleolus to help chemically fine-tune other RNAs, especially ribosomal RNA. As an H/ACA box snoRNA, its general role is to guide a specific type of base modification. The precise targets of this particular snoRNA are not specified here.
SNORA91.technical: SNORA91 is an H/ACA box small nucleolar RNA. Members of this class canonically serve as guide RNAs directing site-specific pseudouridylation of target RNAs (mainly rRNA and snRNA) through base-pairing within the H/ACA snoRNP. No curated functional summary or specific target is provided, so only the general, well-established class function should be assumed; its individual targets are not established here.

SNORD159.simple: This is a small nucleolar RNA, a short non-coding RNA that works in the nucleolus to help chemically modify other RNAs, especially ribosomal RNA, during ribosome assembly. As a C/D box snoRNA, its general role is to guide a particular kind of methylation on target RNA. The exact targets of this snoRNA are not specified here.
SNORD159.technical: SNORD159 is a C/D box small nucleolar RNA. C/D box snoRNAs canonically act as guide RNAs directing site-specific 2'-O-methylation of target RNAs (typically rRNA and snRNA) via base-pairing within the box C/D snoRNP, which includes fibrillarin as the methyltransferase. No curated functional summary or specific target is provided, so only the general class function should be assumed; its individual target sites are not established here.

SNORD74B.simple: This is a small nucleolar RNA, a short non-coding RNA found in the nucleolus that helps chemically modify other RNAs, especially ribosomal RNA, as ribosomes are made. As a C/D box snoRNA, its general role is to guide a particular methylation of target RNA. The specific targets of this snoRNA are not detailed here.
SNORD74B.technical: SNORD74B is a C/D box small nucleolar RNA, predicted to be involved in RNA processing and to localize to the nucleolus. C/D box snoRNAs canonically guide site-specific 2'-O-methylation of target RNAs (mainly rRNA and snRNA) through base-pairing within the box C/D snoRNP containing fibrillarin. The specific target sites for this snoRNA are not provided here, so the general class function should be assumed rather than a particular validated target.

SOD1-DT.simple: This is a non-coding RNA gene located next to the SOD1 gene and read off the DNA in the opposite, divergent direction from it. It does not make a protein, and its specific function is not well understood. Although its neighbor SOD1 is well known in the disease ALS, no functional role for this RNA is established.
SOD1-DT.technical: SOD1-DT (SOD1 divergent transcript) is a long non-coding RNA gene transcribed in the opposite orientation from a shared or nearby promoter region with the protein-coding gene SOD1. No curated functional summary is provided, and its specific molecular role is not well characterized. While SOD1 is a well-studied gene implicated in familial amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, no functional or regulatory role for this divergent transcript is established, and it should be treated as uncharacterized.

SON.simple: SON is a protein-coding gene (not a non-coding RNA) that makes a protein helping the cell correctly splice its messenger RNAs, especially difficult ones with weak splice signals. It also binds DNA and can suppress activity of the hepatitis B virus. Because proper splicing is so important, loss of SON function causes a developmental disorder (ZTTK syndrome).
SON.technical: SON is a protein-coding gene encoding an RNA- and DNA-binding protein containing multiple simple repeats. The protein binds RNA and promotes pre-mRNA splicing, particularly of transcripts bearing weak/poor splice sites, and also recognizes a specific DNA sequence in human hepatitis B virus (HBV) where it represses HBV core promoter activity. Alternative splicing yields multiple transcript variants, and a processed pseudogene exists on chromosome 1; haploinsufficiency of SON causes ZTTK (Zhu-Tokita-Takenouchi-Kim) syndrome, a multisystem neurodevelopmental disorder.

SSR4P1.simple: SSR4P1 is a pseudogene, meaning it is a DNA copy that resembles the working SSR4 gene but is not expected to make a functional product. Pseudogenes are essentially genetic remnants, and this one is not known to have a specific role of its own.
SSR4P1.technical: SSR4P1 is a processed/duplicated pseudogene derived from SSR4 (signal sequence receptor subunit 4). As a pseudogene it is generally not expected to encode a functional protein, and no well-established regulatory function has been documented for this locus. Any low-level transcription, if present, has not been linked to a characterized biological role.

TEKT4P2.simple: TEKT4P2 is a pseudogene, a DNA sequence that looks like the tektin 4 gene but is not expected to produce a working protein. It is best thought of as an inactive genetic relic rather than a functional gene.
TEKT4P2.technical: TEKT4P2 is a pseudogene of TEKT4 (tektin 4), a member of the tektin family of cytoskeletal/ciliary filament proteins. Being a pseudogene, it is typically not expected to yield a functional product, and no validated regulatory role has been established for this copy. Its biological significance, if any, remains uncharacterized.

TIAM1-AS1.simple: TIAM1-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA, a type of RNA that does not make a protein. It is transcribed from the opposite strand of the neighboring TIAM1 gene; its own specific function is not well characterized.
TIAM1-AS1.technical: TIAM1-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed antisense to the protein-coding gene TIAM1. Antisense lncRNAs can in principle influence expression of their cognate sense gene in cis, but a specific, validated mechanism for TIAM1-AS1 has not been firmly established. Its functional role should be regarded as not well characterized.

TMEM50B.simple: TMEM50B provides instructions for a small protein that sits in cell membranes, including the endoplasmic reticulum (a cellular factory for proteins). It is predicted to help route cargo within the cell's internal sorting and transport system. This is a protein-coding gene rather than a non-coding RNA.
TMEM50B.technical: TMEM50B encodes a transmembrane protein localized to the endoplasmic reticulum and is predicted to participate in late endosome-to-vacuole transport via the multivesicular body sorting pathway. It lies on chromosome 21 within the Down syndrome region of interest. This is a protein-coding gene; its precise molecular mechanism is inferred largely from predicted activity rather than direct functional characterization.

TRPM2.simple: TRPM2 makes a channel protein that lets ions such as calcium flow into cells, and it is activated under conditions of oxidative stress. It contributes to processes including cell death signaling and immune responses. This is a protein-coding gene rather than a non-coding RNA.
TRPM2.technical: TRPM2 encodes a member of the transient receptor potential (TRP) melastatin channel family, a non-selective cation channel permeable to Ca2+ that is gated by ADP-ribose and activated under oxidative-stress conditions. It is implicated in oxidative-stress-induced cell death, inflammatory signaling, and immune cell function. Note that the source description/summary provided for this entry ("clusterin") refers to a different gene and does not match the TRPM2 symbol; the summary here is based on the established identity of TRPM2 as a TRP channel.

TRPM2-AS.simple: TRPM2-AS is a long non-coding RNA, an RNA molecule that does not code for a protein. It is read from the opposite strand of the TRPM2 gene; its own specific function is not well established.
TRPM2-AS.technical: TRPM2-AS is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed antisense to the TRPM2 locus. While antisense transcripts can potentially regulate their overlapping sense gene, a definitive, validated mechanism for TRPM2-AS has not been firmly established, and its functional role should be considered not well characterized.

TSPEAR-AS1.simple: TSPEAR-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA that does not make a protein. It is transcribed opposite the TSPEAR gene, and its specific biological role is not well characterized.
TSPEAR-AS1.technical: TSPEAR-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed antisense to TSPEAR. No specific, validated molecular mechanism or disease association has been firmly established for this transcript, so its functional role should be regarded as uncharacterized.

TSPEAR-AS2.simple: TSPEAR-AS2 is a long non-coding RNA, an RNA that does not encode a protein. It is associated with the TSPEAR gene region, and its own specific function is not well characterized.
TSPEAR-AS2.technical: TSPEAR-AS2 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) located in antisense orientation relative to TSPEAR. As with many antisense lncRNAs, a specific validated function has not been established for this transcript, and its biological role remains uncharacterized.

TTC3-AS1.simple: TTC3-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA that does not produce a protein. It is read from the opposite strand of the TTC3 gene on chromosome 21; its specific function is not well characterized.
TTC3-AS1.technical: TTC3-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed antisense to TTC3, a gene situated within the chromosome 21 Down syndrome critical region. No specific, validated mechanism or disease role has been firmly established for TTC3-AS1, so its function should be regarded as not well characterized.

UBASH3A.simple: UBASH3A makes a protein that acts as a brake on T-cell signaling, helping to keep the immune system in check. It can also promote programmed cell death in T cells under certain conditions. This is a protein-coding gene rather than a non-coding RNA.
UBASH3A.technical: UBASH3A encodes a member of the T-cell ubiquitin ligand (TULA) family that negatively regulates T-cell receptor signaling and can facilitate growth-factor-withdrawal-induced apoptosis in T cells, in part through interaction with the apoptosis-inducing factor AIF. Multiple transcript variants arise by alternative splicing. This is a protein-coding gene; variants in UBASH3A have been associated with autoimmune susceptibility in the broader literature, consistent with its immune-regulatory role.

UMODL1-AS1.simple: UMODL1-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA that does not code for a protein. It is transcribed opposite the UMODL1 gene, and its specific function is not well characterized.
UMODL1-AS1.technical: UMODL1-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed antisense to UMODL1. No specific, validated molecular mechanism or disease association has been firmly established for this transcript, so its functional role should be considered uncharacterized.

URB1-AS1.simple: URB1-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA, an RNA that does not make a protein. It is a divergent transcript produced near the URB1 gene, sharing its promoter region; its own specific function is not well characterized.
URB1-AS1.technical: URB1-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) classified as a divergent transcript of URB1, meaning it is transcribed from the opposite strand near a shared bidirectional promoter region. Divergent lncRNAs are often associated with the transcriptional regulation of their neighboring gene, but a specific validated function for URB1-AS1 has not been firmly established and should be regarded as uncharacterized.

VPS26C.simple: VPS26C makes a protein involved in sorting and recycling cargo within the cell's endosome system, the network that routes material to its proper destinations. It lies in a region of chromosome 21 that has been linked to features of Down syndrome. This is a protein-coding gene rather than a non-coding RNA.
VPS26C.technical: VPS26C (formerly DSCR3) encodes an endosomal protein-sorting factor that functions as part of the retriever complex, which mediates endosomal cargo recycling analogous to the related retromer pathway. The gene maps to the chromosome 21q22.2 CBR-ERG region implicated in Down syndrome phenotypes. This is a protein-coding gene; note the provided source summary describes the gene under its older DSCR3 name.

WDR4.simple: WDR4 makes a protein that is part of an enzyme complex which chemically modifies transfer RNAs, the molecules that help build proteins. It works as a partner subunit that helps the complex do its job. This is a protein-coding gene rather than a non-coding RNA.
WDR4.technical: WDR4 encodes a WD40-repeat protein that serves as the non-catalytic subunit of the tRNA N7-guanosine (m7G46) methyltransferase complex, partnering with the catalytic subunit METTL1. WD-repeat proteins facilitate assembly of multiprotein complexes, and WDR4 is required for proper m7G tRNA modification. The gene maps to chromosome 21q22.3; mutations in WDR4 have been associated with microcephalic primordial dwarfism and related neurodevelopmental disorders in the established literature. This is a protein-coding gene.

ZNF295-AS1.simple: ZNF295-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA that does not encode a protein. It is transcribed opposite the ZNF295 (ZBTB21) gene on chromosome 21; its specific function is not well characterized.
ZNF295-AS1.technical: ZNF295-AS1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) transcribed antisense to ZNF295 (also known as ZBTB21), located within the chromosome 21 Down syndrome region. No specific, validated molecular mechanism or disease role has been firmly established for ZNF295-AS1, so its function should be regarded as not well characterized.
